data.title: AI Is Catalyzing A New Era Of Expansion For Alphabet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Two weeks ago, Alphabet Inc. ( GOOG) (GOOGL) (GOOG:CA) delivered an impressive quarter, which, in my opinion, is a key reason why I am reiterating a Buy rating for the company. The Search giant's core advertising business is inflecting upward again. And ironically, this is because of AI. In most of my previous articles on some of its peers, such as Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), I said that AI is a key driver for these giant companies. I have also said that AI will be a key catalyst for driving growth in most of these hyperscalers. Specifically, for GOOG, Google Cloud is accelerating as the next growth pillar. And this is a key point that I addressed in my previous Alphabet article. In Q3 2025 Earnings Report, Alphabet's revenues surged 16% y/y to over $102 Bn. And this is the first time it ever hit a 12 figure quarter. The bottom-line is that AI investments are translating into real business results across search, YouTube and cloud business, boosting both the top line and operating leverage, as noted by the CEO, Sundar Pichai:

...We delivered our first ever $100 billion quarter. Five years ago, our quarterly revenue was at $50 billion. Our revenue number has doubled since then, and we are firmly in the generative AI era. In parallel, we've built for the long term and diversified with successful businesses in cloud, YouTube and subscriptions...

My key takeaway from this is that Alphabet's growth engine is revving at scale and AI is catalyzing a new era of expansion. And this is why I am remaining bullish and reiterating my Buy rating. Because GOOG's combination of rejuvenated ad momentum and booming cloud and AI demand makes the company a unique winner in Big Tech. Yes, the stock may not look cheap on traditional metrics, especially given the fact that it scores an F Valuation grade. Nevertheless, its Growth grade shows a different picture. GOOG scores a B growth grade with a ~13% y/y revenue growth, which is far above the sector median of ~3%, as shown below.

Therefore in my view, paying a premium for a business delivering accelerating double digit growth and expanding margins is justified.

Google is scaling Gemini at consumer and enterprise levels fast. Management actually says that Gemini now processes about 7 Bn tokens per minute via direct API calls. And the Gemini app has over 650Mn monthly active users, and user queries are up 3X from Q3. The team also guided the Gemini 3.0 release which was released on Nov 18th. And this keeps the cadence tight. AI Mode in search is also already live globally in over 40 Languages. And this serves over 40Mn active daily users. Also, AI overviews continue to roll out at scale. And I believe this is exactly what I would want to see. Ship speed, usage and an upgrade path baked in. And when I look at a comparative context, Microsoft (MSFT) leans on OpenAI (OPENAI) models inside Copilot. Amazon (AMZN) on the other side is pushing Bedrock and its Anthropic tie up. Meta (META) is also seeding Llama broadly. Google (GOOG) is literally the only one offering both its own frontier models and the first partly silicon at hyperscale. And this matters when enterprises want one stack for training and serving, as noted by the CEO, Sundar Pichai:

...we are firmly in the generative AI era...

And I believe when the CEO said this, my takeaway is that AI is no longer a science project at GOOG anymore. In fact, it is moving revenue, engagement, and roadmap at the same time.

Business Overview

Alphabet's business spans search, advertising, cloud services, YouTube, hardware, and more. And the company's model is a potent blend of massive scale and diversification. Search and other Google services still drive the bulk of revenue, of course (at $87Bn in Q3 2025 which is a 14y/y growth). Then there are other newer engines like Cloud (at $15.2Bn representing a 34% y/y growth). GOOG has also successfully evolved from a one trick search pony into a multi cylinder tech powerhouse. And all this is while leveraging AI in every part of the stack.

GOOG's core ad revenues are back to solid growth. Search ads jumped 15% y/y and YouTube ad sales also climbed 15% as digital ad spending rebounded. From the Earnings call, management highlighted that AI features in search (such as AI overviews and interactive AI mode) are actually driving more user engagement and queries, especially when it comes to younger users. This counters the bear narrative that generative AI would cannibalize GOOG's search traffic. Instead, AI is making search more useful and sticky creating more opportunities to monetize, as noted by the CEO;

...AI is driving an expansionary moment for Search. As people learn what they can do with our new AI experiences, they're increasingly coming back to Search more...

And clearly from this, in my view, AI enhancements are boosting search usage, and this is more of a tailwind for GOOG's ad business rather than a threat.

When I look at YouTube, it remains a juggernaut in Video. It is now #1 in US streaming, according to Nielsen. It even broke a record with NFL live stream where it had over 17Mn concurrent viewers. Short form YouTube Shorts is also monetizing better. And this is aided by new AI-powered creator tools. My point is that Alphabet's legacy platforms, such as search and YouTube, are not standing still. They are also innovating with AI and continuing to grow and print cash.

Of course, GOOG's business overview would not be complete without mentioning Google Cloud and the broader AI ecosystem it plays in. In my previous GOOG article, I majored on Google Cloud and how it is a game changer for Alphabet. Now, imagine combining Google Cloud with the traditional legacy businesses that are also AI-embedded, and the end result is a powerful business in a new era of expansion. The Cloud segment is a key differentiator. Because it had grown from a negligible contributor almost 5 to 6 years ago to a $53Bn annual run rate business. And Google is the only cloud provider offering both top tier AI chips and proprietary models. It is also winning big customers because 9 out of 10 AI research labs chose Google cloud:

...This leadership is winning customers like HCA Healthcare, LG AI Research and Macquarie Bank, and it's why 9 of the top 10 AI labs choose Google Cloud....

...we are signing new customers faster...

...Over 70% of existing Google Cloud customers use our AI products, including Banco BV, Best Buy and FairPrice Group...

Growth drivers

In my view, the biggest growth driver for GOOG now is its central role in the AI and cloud computing ecosystem. And the clever strategies it is using to extend that advantage. GOOG is not just a bystander in the AI boom but rather a primary enabler of AI across hardware, software, and cloud services. Specifically, I see several opportunities in:

First is AI driven Search & Ads. GOOG has turned the AI threat into an opportunity. New AI search experiences (such as overviews and conversational modes) are actually driving usage and queries. And as these AI features roll out globally, then they should continue expanding Search's reach. More queries and engagements ultimately mean more ad impressions to monetize. And the bottom line is that AI is actually expanding GOOG's core and TAM by unlocking new commercial intent rather than shrinking it.

Secondly is the ecosystem positioning. I say this because GOOG sits at an advantageous nexus in the AI ecosystem. For example, Google is a top client of Nvidia (NVDA) [for GPUs] and a competitor via TPUs. However, this dual approach implies that it wins either way as AI compute demand increases. It also partners with OpenAI (OPENAI) indirectly via NVDA's investments there while also competing via Google DeepMind and Gemini. It is also cooperating with Meta (META) on cloud infrastructure even as Meta develops its own AI models (Llama) that rivals Googles. Also, let me not forget about Amazon (AMZN) which just announced its own $4Bn investment in Anthropic to ensure AWS. This means that AI arms race is quite intense. But Alphabet's strategy of open handed partnerships could make it a central backbone for many AI Ventures.

Alphabet is also investing ahead of demand. Q3 capex was $24Bn and the full year 2025 capex is now guided at between $91Bn to $93Bn. This is about 60% in servers and 40% in data centers and networking. Depreciation rose $1.6Bn y/y to $5.6Bn and I believe this will set up again as new capacity comes online. Management also flagged a tight supply-demand ecosystem, especially moving into 2026. And this is exactly why capex is front loaded. And I believe cash flow can handle it. Because the trailing 12th-month cash flow sits around $73.6Bn even after the step in spending.

When I look at the pipeline, GOOG's cloud reached $155Bn which was up 46% sequentially and 82% y/y. And the team has signed more than $1Bn deals through Q3 than in the last 2 previous years combined, and 13 product lines now run at $1Bn ARR each. New GCP customers rose 34% y/y and 70% of the existing customers already use GOOG's AI products. And the revenue from products built on GOOG's gen AI models grew 200% y/y. This I believe is a healthy order book by any company's yardstick.

It is also important to mention that GOOG is scaling both partner GPU and its own TPUs. The new A4X Max instances powered by Nvidia (NVDA) GB300 are shipping to cloud customers, and GOOG's 7th gen TPU (commonly known as Ironwood) is also coming to general availability. GOOG is also reserving capacity for ecosystem wins. Anthropic plans to access up to 1 Mn TPUs. And I believe this is a visible example of GOOG's full-stack positioning.

Lastly, there is Google Cloud, which I will not focus much on since I covered it in my previous article on GOOG.

Valuation

Like I said before, GOOG's valuation numbers may not look decent as of now; however, I still find them reasonable relative to peers and its own growth. For instance, when I look at the FWD PEG ratio, GOOG stands at 1.85x, which ideally is priced at 1.8 times its Long term earnings growth rate. And this is very similar to Amazon (AMZN) at 1.81x and below Microsoft's (MSFT) 2.36x. Meta's (META) PEG is 1.52x given its faster growth off a lower base.

My point is that Alphabet's PEG of 1.8x suggests that the stock is not pricing in outrageous growth. It is roughly in line with Amazon (AMZN) and at a ~27% discount to Microsoft's (MSFT) PEG.

Again, when I look at FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that GOOG stands at ~27x (almost similar to META's) versus 31x for MSFT and 34x for AMZN. Alphabet is about 10% to 20% cheaper than those peers on FWD earnings. And this shows that Alphabet is trading at a discount relative to its peers. Comparing to the sector median, GOOG is trading at a premium since the median is 15x. So this means that GOOG is trading at a 75% premium to the average Comm Service company. Yet, the sector includes slow growth companies. Nevertheless, for a mega cap like GOOG, which is growing at 30% [EPS] I would say that a 28X FWD Non-GAAP P/E is not excessive in my view.

Alphabet's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for GOOG as of now is that the Gen AI frenzy is a double-edged sword. This means that if alternative AI search or chatbot tools were to start displacing GOOG for certain queries, then it would certainly pressure search usage. However, so far, GOOG's AI enhancement seems to be expanding its moat and not eroding it. However, it is important to note that the landscape can change fast in tech.

Final Thoughts

Alphabet has reasserted itself as a top-tier growth story in Big Tech. And this has been powered by the virtuous cycle of AI driving more usage, which eventually drives more revenue. And funds more AI innovation. And Q3 2025 results underscored that both arms of the business, that is ads and cloud, are thriving. And this is why I am reiterating my Buy rating for Alphabet Inc.

data.title: Tracking David Tepper's Appaloosa Management Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to David Tepper’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Appaloosa Management’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/13/2025. Please visit our Tracking David Tepper’s Appaloosa Management Portfolio series to get an idea of his investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q2 2025.

This quarter, Tepper’s 13F portfolio value increased from ~$6.45B to ~$7.38B. The number of holdings increased from 38 to 45. The top five positions are Alibaba Group Holdings, Amazon, Whirlpool, NVIDIA, and Alphabet. They add up to ~38% of the portfolio.

New Stakes:

Vistra Corp. (VST): VST is a 3.30% of the portfolio stake established this quarter at prices between ~$178 and ~$220. The stock currently trades just below that range at ~$174.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): The ~2% AMD position was purchased this quarter at prices between ~$134 and ~$187, and it is now above that range at ~$206.

Fiserv Inc. (FISV): FISV is a 1.62% of the portion stake established this quarter at prices between ~$127 and ~$176. It is now well below that range at $63.80.

American Airlines (AAL): The 1.41% AAL position was purchased this quarter at prices between ~$11 and ~$14. The stock is now at $12.24.

Truist Financial (TFC), Citizens Financial (CFG), Comerica (CMA), Owens Corning (OC), Western Alliance (WAL), and Zions Bancorp (ZION): These very small (less than ~1% of the portfolio each) stakes were established this quarter.

Stake Disposals:

Intel Corp (INTC): The 2.78% INTC position was purchased during the last quarter at prices between $17.67 and $23.90. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$19 and ~$36. It is now at $33.62.

Oracle Corp (ORCL), and Wynn Resorts (WYNN): These two positions were dropped this quarter.

Stake Increases:

Whirlpool Corp (WHR): WHR is a large (top three) 5.85% stake established during the last quarter at prices between ~$74 and ~$102. There was a ~200% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$77 and ~$112. The stock currently trades below those ranges at $70.25.

NVIDIA Corp (NVDA): NVDA is a 4.80% of the portfolio position primarily built during the last quarter at prices between ~$87 and ~$159. The stock currently trades at ~$181. There was a ~9% stake increase this quarter.

KraneShares China Internet (KWEB): KWEB is a 4.21% of the portfolio position purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$23 and ~$27. There was a ~30% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$26 and ~$32. Q3 2024 saw a ~16% trimming at prices between ~$25 and ~$34. The next quarter saw a ~22% stake increase at prices between ~$29 and ~$37.50. There was a ~13% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$27 and ~$38. This quarter saw a ~85% stake increase at prices between ~$33 and ~$43. The stock currently trades at $36.23.

Taiwan Semi (TSM): The ~4% TSM stake saw a ~275% increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$134 and ~$229. The stock is now at ~$278. This quarter saw a minor ~3% further increase.

Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM): The 2.81% QCOM position was purchased during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$103 and ~$128. There was a ~30% selling in the next quarter at prices between ~$106 and ~$131. That was followed by a ~23% reduction during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$105 and ~$146. Q1 2025 also saw a ~55% selling at prices between ~$148 and ~$177. There was a ~250% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$144 and ~$174. The stock currently trades at ~$160.

Baidu (BIDU), Goodyear Tire & Rubber (GT), iShares China Large Cap ETF (FXI), KE Holdings (BEKE), and Mohawk Industries (MHK): These positions were increased during the quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA): BABA is currently the largest position at ~16% of the portfolio. It was built during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$79 and ~$103. The position was increased by 159% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$68 and ~$78. That was followed by a ~18% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$82 and ~$118. Q1 2025 saw a ~22% reduction at prices between ~$80 and ~$148. That was followed by a similar reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$96 and ~$135. The stock currently trades at ~$153. This quarter also saw a ~9% trimming.

Amazon.com (AMZN): The 7.43% AMZN stake was purchased during Q1 2019 at prices between ~$75 and ~$91. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. Q3 2023 saw a ~20% increase at prices between ~$126 and ~$145 while during Q4 2024 there was a ~20% selling at prices between ~$181 and ~$233. The stock currently trades at ~$217. The last three quarters saw only minor adjustments.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): GOOGL is now at 4.56% of the portfolio. It has been a significant presence in the portfolio since Q1 2012 and the original purchase was at prices between ~$14.50 and ~$16.25. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows: There was a ~20% stake increase during Q3 2023 while the next quarter saw similar selling. That was followed by another ~25% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$143 and ~$183. The stock currently trades at ~$290. This quarter also saw a ~8% trimming.

NRG Energy (NRG): The 4.10% NRG stake was established during Q3 2024 at prices between $68.58 and $90.81. The next quarter saw a ~82% stake increase at prices between $84.55 and $101.17. That was followed by a ~13% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$81 and ~$117. The stock is now well above those ranges at ~$161. There was a ~6% trimming this quarter.

Meta Platforms (META): META is now at 3.68% of the portfolio. It was established during Q3 2016 at prices between $114 and $131 and increased by ~50% in the following quarter at prices between $115 and $133. H2 2017 saw a stake doubling at prices between $148 and $183. The position has since wavered. Recent activity follows. Q1 2025 saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between ~$553 and ~$741. There was a ~27% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$480 and ~$748. The stock is currently at ~$589. This quarter also saw a ~8% trimming.

Microsoft (MSFT): The 3.86% MSFT stake was built during 2020 at prices between $152 and $232. There was a whopping ~375% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$275 and ~$348. Recent activity follows. Q3 2024 saw a ~18% selling at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. That was followed by a ~47% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The stock currently trades at ~$478. They are harvesting gains. There was further trimming during the last two quarters.

PDD Holdings (PDD): PDD is a 3.22% of the portfolio stake that saw the position almost doubled during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$68 and ~$103. The next quarter saw the position sold down by ~45% at prices between ~$98 and ~$150. The stake was increased by 171% during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$110 and ~$151. Q3 2024 saw a whopping ~175% stake increase at prices between $89.17 and $151. There was a ~18% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$94 and ~$133. That was followed by a ~55% reduction during the last quarter at prices between ~$87 and ~$125. The stock is now at ~$113. This quarter also saw a ~10% trimming.

Uber Technologies (UBER): UBER is now at 3.19% of the portfolio. The original large stake was purchased in Q2 2021 at prices between ~$44 and ~$61. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. There was a ~113% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$61 and ~$82. The last quarter saw a ~14% trimming at prices between ~$61 and ~$94. That was followed by a similar reduction this quarter at prices between ~$85 and ~$102. The stock currently trades at $83.36.

JD.com (JD): JD is a ~3% of the portfolio position purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$21.50 and ~$28. The next quarter saw a ~18% stake increase at prices between $25.06 and $35.27. There was a ~70% increase during Q3 2024 at prices between $24.95 and $39.90. That was followed by another ~44% increase during the next quarter at prices between $33.35 and $47.08. Q1 2025 saw a ~23% reduction at prices between ~$33 and ~$47. The last two quarters also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$30 and ~$36. The stock currently trades at $28.39.

ASML Holdings (ASML), Block Inc. (XYZ), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Corning Inc. (GLW), Deutsche Bank (DB), Iqvia Holdings (IQV), L3Harris Technologies (LHX), Lyft Inc. (LYFT), Lam Research (LRCX), Micron Technology (MU), MPLX LP (MPLX), RTX Corporation (RTX), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), and United Airlines (UAL): These stakes were decreased this quarter.

Kept Steady:

Energy Transfer LP (ET): Energy Transfer Partners merged with Energy Transfer Equity and the resulting entity was renamed Energy Transfer LP. The transaction closed during 2018, and terms were 1.28 shares of ETE for each ETP. Tepper held shares in both and those got converted to ET shares. There was a stake doubling in Q4 2019 at prices between $11 and $13. The next three quarters saw the stake again doubled at prices between $4.55 and $13.75 while Q2 2021 saw a ~45% selling at prices between ~$7.70 and ~$11.35. The last year saw further selling. The stock is now at $16.64, and the stake is now very small at 1.15% of the portfolio.

Caesars Entertainment (CZR): The 0.77% CZR stake was kept steady this quarter.

data.title: Carillon Reams Unconstrained Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Tariff uncertainty lingers

During the third quarter, a range of tariffs were implemented, accompanied by the announcement of additional levies, even as bilateral trade agreements with the United Kingdom and Japan were enacted. The complexity and volume of these measures make it difficult to assess their implications. Moreover, the economic impact remains uncertain, particularly following a federal appeals court ruling that deemed many of the tariffs unlawful. While a U.S. Supreme Court decision is anticipated in the fourth quarter, the contested tariffs were enforced, contributing to heightened ambiguity in the broader economic outlook.

Labor market weakness

Nonfarm payroll data released earlier in the year pointed to a moderating labor market. However, August revisions removed nearly 260,000 jobs from the prior two months' totals, significantly altering that initial assessment. The annual benchmark update released in September introduced a larger than anticipated downward revision, further weakening the outlook for this component of the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed) dual mandate. Meanwhile, a contracted labor pool, driven by a decline in foreign-born workers, helped to keep the unemployment rate low. This divergence from payroll data suggests the rate may be artificially suppressed, complicating the Fed's evaluation of labor market conditions.

Inflation remains above target

Services inflation has remained persistently elevated, posing a significant obstacle to achieving the Federal Reserve's 2% inflation target. While goods inflation has yet to reflect the impact of tariffs implemented in August, the potential for temporary price increases, should the tariffs

remain in effect, suggests that further progress toward the target may be limited. Despite the challenges in bringing short-term inflation back in line, long-term inflation expectations remain well-anchored, indicating continued market confidence in the Fed's commitment to price stability.

Fed makes 'insurance cut' to rates and maintains independence

At its September meeting, the Federal Reserve approved a widely anticipated 25-basis point rate cut, which Fed Chair Jerome Powell characterized as an "insurance cut." The move reflects growing concern within the Fed over signs of labor market softening, prompting a preference for easing monetary conditions despite inflation remaining above the 2% target and ahead of any potential inflationary effects from recently implemented tariffs. Meanwhile, ongoing pressure from President Trump was largely disregarded by the committee, as the Fed's institutional framework continues to insulate its policy decisions from direct executive influence.

Spread sectors were positive during the third quarter

High yield (HY) credit led the way again in the third quarter as a "risk-on" mood was evident through much of the quarter. Investment grade (IG) credit, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS) all had positive excess returns. MBS excess returns were notable as they lagged the credit market most of the year but kept pace in the third quarter. The yield curve steepened as the Fed's September rate cut and expectations of another cut in October pushed the front end of the curve lower. The long end moved modestly lower as long-term inflation expectations remained anchored. The curve is now inverted out to the 2-year point but is steeper in slope beyond this point.

Portfolio Review

Rate volatility decreased in the third quarter of 2025, with the ICE BofAML MOVE Index declining to levels not seen since 2022. Compared to the beginning of the quarter, the entire yield curve shifted downward with a more pronounced decline at the short end. This led to a steepening of the curve. The Fund's absolute duration positioning benefited performance during the quarter.

Treasury exposure was the largest positive contributor during the quarter as rates fell across the board. Allocations to MBS, both agency and non-agency were also large contributors as the asset class saw positive carry and notable spread compression throughout the quarter. The focus on the belly of the coupon curve saw positive selection effect as this portion of the curve outperformed. The allocation to both investment grade and high yield corporate securities saw positive performance as spreads tightened throughout the quarter despite being near historically tight levels. ABS exposure saw continued positive performance as the team remains focused on high-quality securities, avoiding credit issues that arose in lower-quality issues throughout the quarter. Structured macro trades continued to provide a tailwind as tactical trades benefiting from declining levels of volatility across the globe were implemented. Limited exposure to CMBS continued to provide a marginal benefit during the quarter. Emerging-market foreign exchange was the sole absolute detractor during the quarter.

The allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities decreased notably during the quarter as the Fund took profits following a sharp tightening of spreads in September. Non-agency mortgages slightly increased during the quarter. The allocation to IG corporates decreased slightly, primarily through a reduction in exposure to industrials. The high yield allocation decreased as opportunities presented themselves to take profits throughout the quarter but remained at a level above the start of the year. ABS exposure was the largest quarter-over-quarter increase as the attractiveness of the opportunity set increased, though it should be noted the exposure was all AAA with no subprime. The weight in CMBS remained flat and continued to reside at a low absolute exposure as this asset class continues to require selectivity. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds decreased and the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies increased, ending the quarter with a small net non-U.S. exposure. The weight in U.S. Treasurys increased during the quarter.

Portfolio duration increased during the quarter as the yield curve steepened. Duration grew to slightly above neutral range, reflecting real rates that are attractive on a long-term basis but acknowledging the possible contribution to the volatility of rates given current market pricing. Yield curve exposure was distributed across the curve with a continued increase in concentration toward the 7- to 10-year duration segment.

Outlook

Tariff impact unknown, but legal ruling anticipated in Q4

The U.S. Supreme Court has agreed to hear arguments concerning the Trump administration's authority to impose tariffs, following an appellate court ruling that found the action unlawful. Should the court uphold the tariffs, the economic implications, particularly for inflation and consumer demand, are likely to unfold gradually, as broad-based levies typically do not produce immediate effects. Conversely, if the appellate court's decision is affirmed, uncertainty will persist. Rebating previously collected tariffs could prove administratively complex, and the fiscal impact may be significant, given that tariff revenues were already allocated to fund enacted spending. Moreover, the administration is expected to explore alternative legal avenues to maintain tariff measures, leaving the ultimate economic impact unresolved.

Fed dot plot points to more cuts

Although the Federal Reserve cites labor market weakness as its primary rationale for easing, financial conditions appeared accommodative even prior to the September rate cut. Inflation risks persist, with current measures remaining above the Fed's 2% target, raising questions about the timing and justification for additional monetary accommodation. Should the anticipated rate cuts fail to materialize, elevated capital market valuations could face increased vulnerability. There is also growing concern that the Fed may be inadvertently easing into a stagflationary environment, where stimulus proves ineffective in spurring growth while exacerbating inflation that is already running above target, potentially resulting in a policy misstep.

Developed market debts grow unabated

Fitch Ratings downgraded France's credit rating to A+ during the third quarter, following Moody's downgrade of the United States in May. Both actions underscored growing concerns over fiscal discipline among developed economies. Persistent, unfunded spending continues to strain the fiscal health of nations still grappling with post-pandemic budgetary imbalances. As global interest rates have risen over the past several years, debt-servicing costs have increasingly crowded out other key budget priorities. While concerns about global investors potentially shunning U.S. Treasurys due to fiscal profligacy remain, similar fiscal deterioration across other developed markets has tempered the relative impact of such risks.

'Everything is going to be OK' is a crowded trade

Current market valuations offer limited compensation for what we view as considerable risks to the prevailing "Goldilocks" narrative. Investor sentiment continues to trend toward optimism, with grow-

ing expectations that economic and market conditions will remain favorable despite a narrowing probability that key risks can be fully avoided. In this environment, investors may be better served by preparing for an increasingly likely setback rather than by relying on the assumption that a low-probability, benign outcome will materialize.

Investment Team

Mark Egan, CFALead Portfolio Manager

Todd Thompson, CFAPortfolio Co-Manager

Clark Holland, CFAPortfolio Co-Manager

Jason Hoyer, CFAPortfolio Co-Manager

Dimitri Silva, CFAPortfolio Co-Manager

Neil AggarwalPortfolio Co-Manager

Characteristics

Total Net Assets(billions): $2.01

Number of holdings: 254

Top 10 Holdings

U.S. Treasury N/B T4 1/8 03/31/32

U.S. Treasury N/B T3 7/8 08/31/32

Secretaria Tesouro(Prices Near 1000)

Letra Tesouro NacionalBltn 0 01/01/26

TSY Infl IX N/B TII2 3/8 02/15/55

U.S. Treasury N/B T4 3/4 08/15/55

U.S. Treasury N/B T3 5/8 09/30/31

FNR 2025-55 FG

FNR 2024-70 FB

Risk Considerations:

The Fund employs an unconstrained investment approach which creates considerable exposure to certain types of securities that present significant volatility in the Fund's performance, particularly over short periods of time. The return of principal in a fixed income fund is not guaranteed. Fixed income funds have the same interest rate, inflation, issuer, maturity and credit risks that are associated with underlying fixed income securities owned by the Fund.

Foreign investments present additional risks due to currency fluctuations, economic and political factors, government regulations, differences in accounting standards and other factors. Investments in emerging markets involve even greater risks.

Mortgage- and Asset-Backed Securities are subject to prepayment risk and the risk of default on the underlying mortgages or other assets.

High-yield securities involve greater risk than investment grade securities and tend to be more sensitive to economic conditions and credit risk.

Derivatives such as options, futures contracts, currency forwards or swap agreements may involve greater risks than if the Fund invested in the referenced obligation directly. Derivatives are subject to risks such as market risk, liquidity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk and management risk. Derivative investments could lose more than the principal amount invested. The Fund may use derivatives for hedging purposes or as part of its investment strategy. The use of leverage, derivatives, and short sales could accelerate losses to the fund. These losses could exceed the amount originally invested.

The Fund may, at times, experience higher than average portfolio turnover, which may generate significant taxable gains and increased trading expenses, which, in turn, may lower the Fund's return.

Short-sale risk includes the potential loss of more money than the actual cost of the investment, and the risk that the third party to the short sale may fail to honor its contract terms, causing a loss to the fund.

Past performance is not indicative of future

results, and investing involves risk, including the risk of loss. All information as of Sept. 30, 2025. Opinions expressed are the current opinions as of the date appearing in this material only. This material should not be construed as research or investment advice. No part of this material may, without Carillon Tower Advisers' prior written consent, be copied, photocopied, or duplicated in any form, by any means.

The information provided should not be construed as a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. The data is shown for informational purposes only and is not indicative of future portfolio characteristics or returns. Portfolio holdings are not stagnant and may change over time without prior notice.

Bond Ratings: Ratings are by Moody's, S&P, and/or Fitch. Ratings provided by nationally recognized statistical rating organizations, also called ratings agencies, are appraisals of a particular issuer's creditworthiness, including the possibility that the issuer will not be able to pay interest or repay principal. Ratings are not recommendations to buy, sell or hold a security, nor do ratings remove market risk. Securities with the same rating can actually trade at significantly different prices. In addition, ratings are subject to review, revision, suspension, reduction or withdrawal at any time, and a rating agency may place an issuer under review or credit watch. Additionally, Fitch reports are available for municipal bonds. More about ratings is available at moody's.com , standardandpoors.com , and fitchratings.com .

Definitions

A+ - Fitch Ratings downgraded France's Long-Term Foreign-Currency Issuer Default Rating to A+ from AA- on Sept. 12, 2025 with an outlook rated as "Stable." Key rating drivers in the downgrade included a high and rising debt ratio, political fragmentation that hinders consolidation, a weak fiscal record, a high 2025 deficit, an uncertain fiscal consolidation path, and fiscal rigidities.

Basis points (BPS) - Measurements used in discussions of interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

Carry - The net return or cost of holding a bond over a specified period of time, based on the

difference in the income it generates and the cost of financing the holding.

Coupon curve - A line that represents the relationship between coupon rates of similar bonds - for example, those with issuers or credit ratings in common - and their time to maturity.

Credit spread - The difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as "bond spreads" or "default spreads," credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the debt security's risk compared with that of Treasury bond - which is considered to be risk-free - and they are considered to be "tighter" when the spread is smaller or "looser" when the spread is larger.

Credit spread tightening - The contraction of credit spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause a decline in credit risk.

Dot plot - A U.S. Federal Reserve chart summarizing the Federal Open Market Committee's (FOMC) outlook for the federal funds rate. Each dot represents the interest rate forecasted by one of the 12 members of the committee.

Dual mandate - Two over-arching goals that influence the course of monetary policy set by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Those goals are maximum employment and stable prices. Maximum employment is defined as the highest level of employment or lowest level of unemployment that the economy can sustain while maintaining a stable inflation rate.

Duration - A measure that incorporates a bond's yield, coupon, final maturity and call features into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Fiscal policy - The tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country's economy.

Foreign exchange, forex, FX - The trading of one currency for another.

High-yield – Bonds with credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor's or below Baa3 from Moody's.

ICE BofAML MOVE Index – A measure of U.S. interest rate volatility that tracks the movement in U.S. Treasury yield volatility implied by current prices of one-month over-the-counter options on 2-year, 5-year, 10-year and 30-year Treasuries.

Inflation target – The rate of price increases that the U.S. Federal Reserve prefers to see to ensure the economy will remain stable. Generally, the Fed's target rate is 2%, as measured by the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index.

Investment-grade – refers to fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Investment-grade – Fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Monetary policy – The decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy's growth, inflation, or employment levels.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Payroll report, officially known as the Employment Situation Summary – A monthly U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) report tracking nonfarm payroll employment and the national unemployment rate, with data on changes in average hourly earnings, and job trends in public and private sectors of employment. The report is based on surveys of households and employers.

Positioning – Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Real interest rate – An interest rate adjusted to remove the effects of inflation. Once adjusted, it reflects the real cost of funds to a borrower and the real yield to a lender or to an investor.

Risk-on – Market sentiment that is typically fueled

by a strong growth environment in which good news supports a bullish outlook and investor expectations of favorable risk/reward ratios.

Securitized products – Products, such as asset-backed securities (ABS) and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), that are created by pooling loans from a variety of sources and issuing bonds that are backed by these loans. Creditworthiness stems from the credit quality of the underlying loans, as opposed to corporate bonds in which creditworthiness is derived from the earning power of the issuing company.

Spread compression – Takes place when a credit spread narrows. This often happens when strong demand brings down the yield on a higher-yielding bond. A credit spread is the difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as “bond spreads” or “default spreads,” credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the risk of the debt security being compared with the Treasury bond, which is considered to be risk-free. Higher quality securities have a lower chance of the issuer defaulting. Lower quality securities have a higher chance of the issuer defaulting.

Spread sector – Nongovernmental fixed-income securities, such as corporate bonds or mortgage bonds, whether investment grade or high yield, that provide an additional yield, or “spread,” over the yield of a risk-free government bond.

Stagflation – An economic condition, first described after the oil shocks of the 1970s, characterized by slow economic growth (or even declines in gross domestic product), relatively high unemployment, and inflation.

Tactical trading – Relatively short-term investing decisions made in response to expected trends or changes in the market based on fundamental and technical analysis.

Tailwind – Events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment's performance.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Valuation – The current or projected worth of an investment. Absolute valuations reflect the absolute value of an investment considered singly, and relative valuations consider the value of an investment against comparable investments.

Yield curve – A line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. A steepening yield curve results from a widening in the difference between short- and long-term interest rates. Investors and market analysts watch certain yield curves for signs of inversion, when yields for longer-term debt instruments fall below yields on short-term debt with the same credit quality. The front or short end of the yield curve refers to groups of bonds with the shortest maturity dates. The long end of the curve refers to bonds with maturities of 10 years or more.

Benchmark Index

The ICE BofA US 3-Month Treasury Index measures the performance of a single issue of outstanding treasury bill which matures closest to, but not beyond, three months from the rebalancing date. The issue is purchased at the beginning of the month and held for a full month; at the end of the month that issue is sold and rolled into a newly selected issue.

data.title: PGIM Target Date Funds Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly Performance Attribution Highlights as Of 9/30/2025

The third quarter was a good one for the capital markets and for target date fund investors. U.S. stocks continued to rally upward and fixed income also delivered positive returns. On the back of strong capital market returns, absolute performance was strong for the PGIM Target Date Funds, with performance ranging from 6.7% in the 2070 Fund to 3.4% in the Income Fund.

Relative to the shorter-dated funds in the series, higher equity exposure benefited the performance of the longer-dated funds (those furthest from their target retirement dates), as stocks significantly outperformed bonds in the quarter. Small-cap stocks led the U.S. stock market in the quarter, returning 12.4% (Russell 2000 Index (IWM)), while large-cap stocks (S&P 500 Index (SPY)) delivered a strong 8.1% for the period. Non-U.S. stocks also delivered strong gains, with emerging markets stocks (MSCI EM Index (EEM)) returning 10.6% and developed markets (MSCI EAFE Index (EFA)) returning 4.8%. The longer-dated funds in series not only have higher equity exposure than the shorter-dated funds but also have higher percentage of total equity exposure allocated to both small-cap U.S. stocks and emerging markets stocks

Bonds ended the quarter in positive territory, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG) posting a 2.0% return. Inflation-protected bonds modestly outpaced the broader bond market, with a 2.1% third quarter return (Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index (TIP)). Relative to the longer- dated funds in the series, the shorter-dated funds have higher allocations to bonds, and a higher percentage of overall bond exposure allocated to TIPS.

Commodities and real estate delivered quarterly returns that were in-between stocks and bonds, with commodities returning 3.7% (Bloomberg Commodity Index (DBC)) and real estate returning 4.1% (FTSE EPRA Nareit Developed Index (RWX)).

Most of the PGIM Target Date Fund underperformed their respective custom benchmarks in the third quarter, with the longer-dated funds underperforming their respective custom benchmarks by a wider margin than the shorter-dated funds. The PGIM Jennison Small Cap Core Equity Fund significantly underperformed its benchmark, which detracted from PGIM TDF returns relative to custom benchmarks, particularly in the longer-dated funds. Benefiting relative results were the strong results from underlying PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund and the PGIM Total Return Bond Fund.

Quarterly Economic Overview

While the year began with heightened market volatility following the second Trump administration's return, uncertainty has eased, as bilateral trade agreements reduced economic policy unpredictability. However, the labor market shows signs of strain. Recent jobs data disappointed, with flat non-farm payrolls since April and a major downward revision to employment growth, revealing that the Bureau of Labor Statistics had overestimated job gains by nearly one million from March 2024-2025. Despite hiring stagnation, layoffs remain low and real wage growth is steady. The Federal Reserve's (Fed) Q3 Survey of Professional Forecasters projects 1.6% GDP growth over the next year--slower, but not recessionary. Core CPI rose 3.1% year-over-year in August, keeping inflation in the public spotlight. Nevertheless, the Fed lowered rates by 25bps in September to 4.00-4.25%, prioritizing labor market risks. Further cuts are expected through 2026, though political pressure for deeper easing is mounting. Unlike the U.S., the Eurozone is closer to its inflation target, allowing the European Central Bank greater flexibility to cut rates compared to the Fed. In contrast, the Bank of Japan is expected to hike rates, as mounting inflation alongside strong GDP growth has set the stage for a hawkish shift in monetary policy.

Trade policy uncertainty eased after President Trump delayed tariff deadlines and secured deals with key partners. However, the Supreme Court will review the legality of 2025 tariffs, potentially invalidating over 70% of them and reducing the effective rate from 17.4% down to 6.8%. This could significantly alter trade dynamics. A temporary U.S.-China trade truce is in place, but elevated tariffs are dampening Chinese retail and industrial activity, posing risks to global growth. While the macro environment remains pro-risk, major leadership changes in France, the UK, and Japan have contributed to bond market volatility and policy uncertainty across major economies.

Market Commentary

U.S. Equity

U.S. equities continued their advance in the third quarter of 2025, with the S&P 500 returning 8.1% on a total return basis, bringing the year-to-date return to 17.6%. Index performance was driven primarily by Information Technology (13.1%) and Communication Services (12.0%), although ten of the 11 sectors posted gains during the quarter. Consumer Staples was the exception, declining by -2.4%. Financial markets shifted back to risk-on mode in Q3, with volatility easing significantly. The VIX averaged 16, down from 24 in Q2, while the MOVE Index fell to 83 from 104, reflecting a calmer environment for risk assets. Equities rallied strongly, with U.S. small caps outperforming large caps. International stocks posted solid gains. Second-quarter earnings growth for the S&P 500 surged to around 13% year over year, strongly exceeding expectations. Positive revisions suggest continued strength, with an improvement in the broader market earnings, while Magnificent Seven growth has started to moderate from its earlier scorching pace.

International Equity

International equity markets rose in Q3 of 2025, with developed and emerging markets posting respective total returns of 4.8% and 10.6% in U.S. dollar terms. Developed markets saw broad-based gains, with a particularly strong quarter in Spain. Positive returns in emerging markets were driven largely by China. U.S. earnings exceptionalism persists, with growth in other developed markets expected to range between 6-8%, compared to 13% year-over-year U.S. growth in Q2 of this year. Meanwhile, earnings in emerging markets have been revised lower over the past year, particularly in Asia, reflecting a more challenging outlook.

Non-Traditional

Even after recent gains, U.S. REITs look attractively priced, trading at about a 4.5% discount to NAV. Earnings growth may be expected to be strong through 2026, as the FFO multiple spread between the S&P 500 and REITs is near its highest point since the Global Financial Crisis. Furthermore, with these favorable valuations and improving fundamentals, private equity activity in the REIT market could pick up over the next year. The Bloomberg Commodity Index increased by 3.7% in the quarter. Returns in the period were driven by gains from Precious Metals (+19%), Softs (+11%) and Livestock (+9%)s, while Grains and Energies declined over the quarter (-4% and -3%, respectively).

Fixed Income

The broad U.S. fixed income index continued to climb in Q3, building on upward momentum so far in 2025. U.S. Treasury yields were supported by rising rate-cut expectations and Fed Chair Powell's dovish stance at Jackson Hole, both of which helped reinforce market confidence. Bond yields may decline in the short term due to rate-cut prospects, but long-term rates face upward pressure from inflation and supply concerns. Credit remains appealing amid moderate growth and attractive nominal yields. For the quarter, on a total-return basis as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, bonds returned 2.0% and U.S. TIPS increased 2.1%.

Performance Review: Quarterly Asset Allocation Effects

The third quarter was a good one for the capital markets and for target date fund investors. U.S. stocks continued to rally upward and fixed income also delivered positive returns. The small-cap segment led the surge in U.S. stocks, outperforming both large-cap and mid- cap stocks in the quarter. Outside of the U.S., emerging markets stocks outpaced the developed markets. The mix of equity exposure, as well as the overall allocations to stocks versus bonds, impacted absolute returns from vintage to vintage.

U.S. small-cap stocks posted a 12.4% gain in the quarter (Russell 2000 Index), outpacing large-cap stocks (S&P 500 Index: 8.1%) and mid-cap stocks (S&P MidCap 400 Index: 5.6%). Non-U.S. developed markets, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, returned 4.8% and emerging markets, as measured by the MSCI EM Index, returned 10.6%.

The longer-dated funds in the series (those furthest from their target retirement dates) have higher equity exposure than the shorter- dated funds, and they also have a higher percentage of total equity exposure allocated to small-cap and emerging markets stocks. The longer-dated funds benefited from their higher equity allocations in general and, relative to the shorter-dated funds, also benefited more from higher allocations to small-cap and emerging markets stocks.

The broad bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, delivered a 2.0% return in the third quarter, in line with the 2.1% return posted by inflation-protected bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index. The shorter-dated funds in the series have higher bond allocations than the longer-dated funds, and they also have a higher percentage of overall bond exposure allocated to TIPS. Relative to the longer-dated funds, shorter-dated fund performance was held back by overall higher bond exposure.

Commodities and real estate play important diversification roles in the PGIM Target Date Funds. In the third quarter, the impact of those exposures was somewhat muted, as both asset classes outperformed bonds, but lagged equities. Commodities, as measured by the Bloomberg Commodity Index, posted a 3.7% return in the quarter. Real estate, as measured by the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index, delivered a 4.1% return.

Underlying Fund Gross Performance Commentary as Of 9/30/2025

U.S. Equity

PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, in the third quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, in the third quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Index Fund performed in line with its benchmark, the S&P MidCap 400 Index, in the third quarter.

PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund  The PGIM Jennison Small-Cap Core Equity Fund significantly underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, in the third

quarter. Security selection within the industrials, information technology and energy sectors detracted the most from relative results. On the positive side, stock selection within the financials and health care sectors added value.

International Equity

PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions International Developed Markets Index Fund performed in line with the MSCI EAFE Index in the third quarter.

PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions Emerging Markets Equity Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, in the third quarter.

Strong relative results were driven by positioning within the materials sector, where an overweight to China was beneficial. From a country perspective, Taiwan was the best performer, followed by Indonesia and Mexico. From a factor perspective, performance was mixed. Growth and top-down factors contributed positively to relative results, while value and equity factors detracted.

Non-Traditional

PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund

The PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies Fund outpaced its benchmark index, the Bloomberg Commodity Index, in the third quarter.

Positive relative results in the quarter were driven by underweight positions in natural gas and soybean oil. Offsetting some of the positive contributions to relative returns was an underweight position in gold, which surged in the quarter.

PGIM Global Real Estate Fund

The PGIM Global Real Estate Fund lagged its benchmark, the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Real Estate Index, in the third quarter.

The Fund's underperformance in the quarter was driven by the North America region. Underperformance in the region stemmed from weak stock selection in the triple net and residential sectors. Europe, on the other hand, contributed positively to relative results in the period, driven primary by stock selection in France and Spain. The Asia Pacific region also outperformed for the period, led by strong stock selection in Australia.

Fixed Income

PGIM TIPS Fund

The PGIM TIPS Fund performed in line with the Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index for the third quarter.

Relative value trades provided positive contributions to relative results, while yield curve and duration positioning had a negligible impact for the period. The Fund ended the quarter with a neutral duration position versus the Index.

PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund

The PGIM Core Conservative Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the third quarter.

Overall sector allocation and security selection decisions both contributed positively to relative performance over the period. Overweight positions to the MBS and non-agency CMBS AAA sectors, as well as an underweight position to U.S. investment grade corporates, were the largest contributors from a sector allocation perspective. Security selection in U.S. investment grade corporates and on-agency CMBS AAA sectors also benefited the Fund's relative results in the quarter. From a market perspective, having less risk than the Fund, on average, detracted from relative results.

PGIM Total Return Bond Fund

The PGIM Total Return Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, in the third quarter.

Overall sector allocation was the largest contributor to relative results in the quarter. Benefiting results were overweight positions in CLO AAAs, non-agency CMBS AAAs and emerging markets high yield sectors, as well as an underweight position to investment grade corporates. Overall security selection also contributed to relative results, with selection in U.S. investment grade corporates, MBS, non-agency CMBS AA- and below, U.S. high yield corporates and non-agency CMBS AAAs contributing the most. On average, having more market risk in the Fund than in the benchmark contributed to performance in quarter. The Fund's duration positioning also contributed, as yields on the front end of the U.S. Treasury curve declined.

Pgim Target Date Funds Overview

The PGIM Target Date Funds combine PGIM's asset management capabilities, insights into participant behavior, and Prudential's leadership in pension risk transfer. Together, these provide us with a unique vantage point to assess the retirement challenge, understand its complexities, and offer a target date fund solution designed to work under the broadest set of circumstances.

The PGIM Target Date Funds are a suite of target date funds that offers:  A differentiated, three-stage glidepath designed to solve for the right risks at the right time, seeking to improve outcomes by mitigating key risks participants face as they prepare for retirement.  Exposure to a diversified array of asset classes, including equities, fixed income, and non- traditional asset classes like TIPS, commodities, and real estate.  A combination of active and passive investment management strategies designed to reduce costs while adding value in areas with greater alpha opportunities.

Portfolio Managers: Jeremy Stempien Joel Kallman, CFA David Blanchett, PhD, CFA, CFP

A Differentiated Glidepath - Solves for The Right Risks at The Right Time

1 Accumulation stage: Higher equity exposure when the greatest risk may be not taking enough risk

2 Preservation stage: Lower equity exposure when participants may have the most to lose and don't have time to recover losses

3 Inflation rotection stage: Focus on assets that may help preserve purchasing power for 30+ years in retirement

The Fund's allocation is ratcheted annually along the glidepath. The subadviser will review the glidepath and allocations annually, at its discretion. Additionally, the Fund's subadviser will review the current Glidepath and the Fund's asset allocations to Underlying Funds annually to determine, in its discretion, whether the then current Glidepath allocations remain appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective. Based on such reviews, the subadviser may, without prior notification to shareholders, make changes to the current Glidepath and/or the Fund's asset allocations as it deems appropriate to meet the Fund's investment objective in light of market and economic conditions and such other factors as it deems relevant. There is no assurance that the Fund's objective will be achieved. The Fund's allocations to the broad asset classes (equity, fixed income, and non-traditional) as set forth in the current Glidepath are not expected to vary from the Fund's allocations set forth in the strategic Glidepath by more than plus or minus 5%.
